location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:166 (doResolveEscalation fallbackArgs, '--decision') and :247-250 (doRecordHumanGate args, '--evidence') / src/shared/orky-action-validate.ts:26-35 (validateFeatureSlug), :47-59 (requireString/requireNonEmptyString) â€” REQ-008, REQ-010, REQ-011
claim: The dispatcher passes human-authored free text (`decision` on the gatekeeper resolve-escalation fallback path, `evidence` on recordHumanGate) directly as a raw, un-encoded CLI argument VALUE to Orky's own gatekeeper CLI. Read C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/cli.js's `parseArgs` (lines 30-41): it scans the ENTIRE argv array for any token starting with `--`, not merely the token immediately following a recognized flag name, and if the token FOLLOWING a `--flag` also starts with `--`, it sets that flag's value to boolean `true` (does NOT consume it) and then re-scans the following token itself as an independent new flag. Because `decision`/`evidence` are always the LAST element of their respective argv arrays (`['resolve-escalation','--feature',featureDir,'--id',escalationId,'--decision',decision]` and `['record','--feature',featureDir,'--gate',gate,'--verdict',verdict,'--evidence',evidence]`), any value beginning with `--` is reinterpreted as a CLI flag instead of being carried through as the literal text. Neither `orky-action-validate.ts`'s `validateResolveEscalationRequest`/`validateRecordHumanGateRequest` nor the dispatcher itself reject or sanitize a `decision`/`evidence` string starting with `--`. I reproduced this adversarially by copying `parseArgs` verbatim from `C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/cli.js` and feeding it the exact argv the dispatcher constructs (command run in this session, not against any real project): (1) `evidence:'--force'` for `recordHumanGate` with `gate:'human-review', verdict:'pass'` parses to `{ ..., evidence: true, force: true }` â€” i.e. the human's `evidence` text is silently DROPPED (replaced by boolean `true`) and the literal `--force` flag is injected into gatekeeper's own `record(feature, gate, verdict, evidence, force)` call (`cli.js:91`: `a.force === true || a.force === 'true'` evaluates to `true`), even though `orky-action-dispatcher.ts`'s own code/tests (TEST-242) assert 'the invocation never includes --force' â€” that assertion is about the literal ARRAY the dispatcher constructs, not about what the CLI's parser ultimately derives from a value inside that array, so it does not catch this. (2) `decision:'--anything is fine'` for the `resolveEscalation` gatekeeper fallback parses to `{ ..., decision: true, 'anything is fine': true }` â€” `gatekeeper.js:627` then executes `esc.decision = decision || ''` with `decision === true`, so `state.json`'s recorded `escalations[].decision` field is silently corrupted to the boolean `true` instead of the human's actual decision text. Impact: (a) DATA INTEGRITY â€” the human decision/evidence text this feature exists to durably and accurately record (the enterprise-arch concern, REQ-013's whole rationale) is silently lost/replaced for any input starting with `--`, which is not a contrived edge case (e.g. a reviewer typing '--- see PR #42 ---' or literally describing a CLI flag in their reasoning); (b) DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH DEGRADATION for REQ-008 â€” the spec explicitly requires the gate restriction to be enforced 'independent of (and in addition to) gatekeeper.js's own enforcement' specifically as defense-in-depth, and requires the invocation to 'NEVER pass --force'; this finding shows attacker/user-controlled `evidence` text CAN cause `--force` to reach `gatekeeper record` regardless of the dispatcher's own hard-coded args array. It is currently INERT only because `gate` is independently pre-validated to `{brainstorm,human-review}` before the CLI ever runs (so `record()`'s `!HUMAN_GATES.has(gate) && !force` bypass check is never reached in the false/true branch that matters) â€” i.e. safety today rests on ONE remaining correct layer, not the two independent layers REQ-008 says it wants; a future regression in the gate pre-check (the exact class of bug reviews exist to catch) would make this a live human-gate-restriction bypass, not merely a data-corruption bug. `feature`/`escalationId` are also passed as raw argv values (`--feature <slug>` in the feedback emit path, `--id <escalationId>` in the gatekeeper fallback) and are similarly reinterpretable if they start with `--`, though lower-likelihood in practice (`feature` must already resolve to an EXISTING `.orky/features/<slug>` directory before any CLI runs, and `--payload`'s JSON content was verified, by the same repro, to survive intact even when `feature` is corrupted â€” so this narrower sub-case mainly causes wrong feature-attribution in the emitted feedback event, not further injection). `title`/`detail`/`phase` (submitWork) are unaffected because they are always carried inside the JSON-encoded `--payload` argument, never as raw argv.
fix: Before constructing any argv array passed to `runOrkyCli`, reject (return `errorKind:'invalid-args'`, CONV-001-actionable) any free-text field that will be placed as a RAW (non-JSON-encoded) CLI argument value if it starts with `--`: specifically `decision` and `escalationId` in `validateResolveEscalationRequest`, `evidence` in `validateRecordHumanGateRequest`, and `feature`'s slug in `validateFeatureSlug` (all four flow into a raw argv position in `orky-action-dispatcher.ts`). A minimal, precise guard is `if (value.startsWith('--')) return { ok:false, error: '<field> must not start with "--" (would be misread as a CLI flag)' }` in each of those validators, or centralize it as a shared `rejectFlagLike(field, value)` helper reused by all of them. Add a regression test asserting `evidence:'--force'` and `decision:'--anything'` (and a `feature`/`escalationId` value starting with `--`) are rejected with `invalid-args` before any CLI is spawned. Longer-term, note in `docs/features/orky-action-dispatch.md` that any FUTURE raw (non-JSON) argv field added to this dispatcher must go through the same guard, since `execFile`'s argument-array safety (REQ-010) prevents SHELL injection but does not prevent the downstream CLI's own naive flag-scanning `parseArgs` from reinterpreting a value that looks like a flag â€” a distinct vulnerability class (CWE-88, argument injection) that the array-based invocation alone does not close.
resolution: Verified fixed. src/shared/orky-action-validate.ts now has a shared `rejectFlagLike(field, value)` helper (`if (value.startsWith('--')) return {ok:false, error:"<field> must not start with '--' (reserved for CLI flags)"}`) wired into: `validateFeatureSlug` (covers `feature` for all four actions via `requireFeatureSlug`/the optional-feature branch in `validateSubmitWorkRequest`), `validateResolveEscalationRequest`'s `escalationId` and `decision` checks, and `validateRecordHumanGateRequest`'s optional `evidence` check. Traced every raw-argv call site in src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts that the original finding named: the resolveEscalation feedback-emit `--feature` (line 151), the gatekeeper-fallback `--id`/`--decision` (line 166), submitWork's optional emit `--feature` (line 202), and recordHumanGate's optional `--evidence` (line 249) -- all five now read from `v.req` (the validated struct), and every one of doResolveEscalation/doSubmitWork/doRecordHumanGate/doDriveStatus calls its validator FIRST and returns `invalid-args` immediately on `!v.ok`, so there is no code path where an unvalidated raw string reaches `runOrkyCli`. `gate`/`verdict` need no textual guard: `gate` is constrained to the fixed 2-element `HUMAN_GATES` set (`{brainstorm,human-review}`) by the dispatcher's own REQ-008 check before the args array is ever built, and `verdict` is enum-checked to exactly `'pass'|'fail'` in the validator -- neither can ever be attacker-influenced free text. `projectRoot` (used raw as `--app <projectRoot>`) isn't flag-guarded either, but is structurally safe: `checkRoot()` requires it to normalize-match an entry in `registry.roots()`, and every registered root passed through feature 0005's `validateRegistryRoot` -> `path.resolve()`, which always returns a filesystem-rooted absolute path (drive-letter or POSIX `/` prefix) and can therefore never start with `--`. Independently re-verified the exploit is closed by re-reading C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/cli.js's `parseArgs` (lines 30-41, unchanged) and C:/dev/Orky/plugin/feedback/cli.js's `parseArgs` (lines 26-37, same vulnerable shape) -- both scan for any whole argv token with `a.startsWith('--')`; `rejectFlagLike`'s `value.startsWith('--')` check exactly matches that recognition threshold (values containing but not starting with `--`, e.g. `'ok --force'`, are a single argv element under array-form `execFile` and are never re-split, so they were never actually reachable -- the guard is neither over- nor under-inclusive). Confirmed `evidence:'--force'` and `decision:'--anything is fine'` are now rejected with `invalid-args` by the validator before any CLI spawns, closing both the data-integrity loss and the REQ-008 defense-in-depth gap the finding described. Ran the frozen regression suite (TEST-283..290, plus the full validator/dispatcher/cli-locate suites, 87 tests) -- all pass, including TEST-290's negative/scope assertion that submitWork's `title`/`detail`/`phase` MAY start with `--` and are still accepted; confirmed this is CORRECT, not just passing, because those three fields travel only inside the `JSON.stringify(...)`-encoded `--payload` argument (which itself always starts with `{`, never `--`), never as their own raw argv element, so guarding them would be over-broad. No gaps found.

claim: The exact same 3-line helper `function isPlainObject(v: unknown): v is Record<string, unknown> { return typeof v === 'object' && v !== null && !Array.isArray(v) }` is independently defined THREE times across this feature's own files: once in the pure `src/shared/orky-action-result.ts` (guards the parsed CLI stdout JSON), once in the pure `src/shared/orky-action-validate.ts` (guards the raw request object), and once again in the main-process `src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts` (guards the raw request object inside `extractString`/`summarizeArgs` for the audit log). A repo-wide grep confirms no pre-existing shared `isPlainObject` utility exists elsewhere that this feature could have reused instead â€” all three are net-new to feature 0007. Two of the three copies (`orky-action-result.ts` and `orky-action-validate.ts`) are both already `src/shared/` pure modules, so the duplication isn't even justified by a main/renderer or pure/impure boundary.
fix: Extract the helper once â€” e.g. a tiny new `src/shared/is-plain-object.ts` (or have `orky-action-dispatcher.ts` and `orky-action-result.ts` both import it from `orky-action-validate.ts`, which is already a dependency of the dispatcher) â€” and delete the other two copies. Purely mechanical, no behavior change; add nothing to the public API surface.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-audit.ts:21 (action: string) and :28 (errorKind?: string) in OrkyActionAuditRecord; src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:333 (private recordAudit(action: string, ...)); compare src/shared/orky-action-result.ts:16 (export type DispatchAction = 'resolveEscalation'|'submitWork'|'recordHumanGate'|'driveStatus', already imported transitively) and src/shared/types.ts:403-413 (OrkyActionErrorKind union) and .orky/features/0007-orky-action-dispatch/03-plan.md TASK-005 ("errorKind?: OrkyActionErrorKind")
claim: `OrkyActionAuditRecord.action` and `OrkyActionAuditRecord.errorKind` are both typed as bare `string` even though a narrower literal-union type already exists for each concept and is used everywhere else in the feature: `DispatchAction` (the exact 4 action names, defined in `orky-action-result.ts` and used to type-check every `mapCliRunToResult(action, ...)` call site) and `OrkyActionErrorKind` (the 10-member discriminator used throughout `OrkyActionResult.errorKind`). `orky-action-dispatcher.ts`'s own `recordAudit(action: string, ...)` parameter is called with only the 4 literal action names today, and `record.errorKind = result.errorKind` assigns a real `OrkyActionErrorKind` value in, but the field's declared type silently widens it to `string`. This is also a direct divergence from the plan: 03-plan.md TASK-005 pins the audit record shape as `errorKind?: OrkyActionErrorKind` verbatim, not `errorKind?: string`. Net effect: a typo'd action name or errorKind string passed to `recordAudit`/into the audit record in a future edit would compile silently instead of being caught, for a log whose entire purpose (REQ-013, enterprise-arch attributability) depends on exactly these fields being reliably one of the known values.
fix: Import `DispatchAction` from `@shared/orky-action-result` and use it for both `OrkyActionAuditRecord.action` and `recordAudit`'s `action` parameter; import `OrkyActionErrorKind` from `@shared/types` (already imported by `orky-action-dispatcher.ts` indirectly via `OrkyActionResult`) and use it for `OrkyActionAuditRecord.errorKind`, matching the plan's own TASK-005 type spec exactly.

claim: The literal object shape `{ ok: false, path: null, dispatched: false, errorKind: <X>, error: <Y> }` (the uniform pre-CLI dispatcher-level rejection per REQ-002/REQ-014) is written out by hand, byte-for-byte identical apart from the errorKind/error values, 17 separate times across the four `do*` action methods â€” once each for `invalid-args` (x4), `root-not-allowed` (x4), the `feature-not-found`-shaped `fdir` failure (x4), `orky-cli-not-found` (x4, plus a 5th inline variant at line 164 covered by FINDING-QUAL-005), and once for `gate-not-allowed` (x1, doRecordHumanGate only). The task brief for this review specifically asked whether the validate-then-checkRoot-then-resolveFeatureDir-then-locateCli sequence's repetition across the four actions is acceptable because each action's exact sequence legitimately differs (per REQ-006/007/008/009) â€” the SEQUENCING does legitimately differ (confirmed: different checks run in different orders/counts per action), but the REJECTION OBJECT SHAPE at each step does not differ at all, and is exactly the kind of boilerplate a shared helper collapses without hiding any of the real per-action logic. This is the single largest, most complex file in the feature (per the review brief's own framing) and is exactly where a future edit (e.g. adding a new field required on every dispatcher-level rejection, such as a request-id for tracing) has the most surface area to update inconsistently â€” 17 near-duplicate literal sites is a lot to keep in sync by hand.
fix: Add a small private helper, e.g. `private reject(errorKind: OrkyActionErrorKind, error: string): OrkyActionResult { return { ok: false, path: null, dispatched: false, errorKind, error } }`, and replace all 17 call sites (`return { ok: false, path: null, dispatched: false, errorKind: 'invalid-args', error: v.error }` becomes `return this.reject('invalid-args', v.error)`, etc.). This does not change any returned value and does not touch the FROZEN test suite's assertions (same resulting object shape).

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:155,159,164,170,172 (doResolveEscalation), :208,212,222 (doSubmitWork), :254,256 (doRecordHumanGate) â€” 10 `as OrkyActionResult` casts; contrast :279-282 (doDriveStatus's structurally identical return literals, which need NO cast)
claim: Every return statement inside the three mutating actions' `this.queue.run(featureDir, async () => {...})` callbacks is force-cast with `as OrkyActionResult` (10 occurrences). `doDriveStatus`, which returns structurally identical object literals (same `{ok, path, dispatched, errorKind?, error?, data?, exitCode?}` shape) directly from the method body rather than from inside a `queue.run(...)` callback, needs no such cast (lines 279-282) â€” because those literals sit in a position with contextual typing from the method's own `Promise<OrkyActionResult>` return-type annotation, so TypeScript checks each literal against the real interface (e.g. `path: 'gatekeeper'` is checked against `OrkyActionPath`). The `queue.run(async () => {...})` callbacks have no such contextual type (the callback is passed as a bare `() => Promise<T>` with `T` inferred structurally from the literals themselves, and `queue.run`'s generic `<T>` return type is never pinned to `OrkyActionResult` at the call site), so without the `as` cast a literal such as `path: 'feedback'` would infer as the widened `string` and fail to satisfy `OrkyActionPath` â€” the cast is compensating for a missing type annotation rather than expressing an intentional narrowing. Net effect: TypeScript is NOT actually verifying that these 10 object literals conform to `OrkyActionResult` at compile time (a typo'd property name or a value of the wrong type inside one of these literals would be silently accepted), for exactly the branches whose `ok`/`errorKind`/`path` shape correctness CONV-001 and REQ-002 depend on most.
fix: Give the `queue.run(...)` callbacks an explicit return-type annotation, e.g. `this.queue.run(featureDir, async (): Promise<OrkyActionResult> => { ... })`, which lets each return statement's object literal be checked contextually against the real interface exactly like `doDriveStatus`'s already are â€” then remove all 10 `as OrkyActionResult` casts. No behavior change; strictly restores compile-time verification of these result shapes.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:162-165 (doResolveEscalation's gatekeeper-fallback CLI-not-found branch) vs. every other orky-cli-not-found return site (:148, :193, :244, :274); tests/main/orky-action-dispatcher.test.ts:380-405 (TEST-252/253, REQ-012's only orky-cli-not-found coverage)
claim: 02-spec.md REQ-012 states the uniform shape for a CLI-not-found rejection as `{ ok:false, path:null, dispatched:false, errorKind:'orky-cli-not-found', error:... }` (path:null, literally), and every one of the four other orky-cli-not-found return sites in this file (lines 148, 193, 244, 274) matches that exactly. The fifth site â€” inside doResolveEscalation's queue.run callback, reached when the FIRST feedback emit succeeds with mode:'noop' (feedback disabled) and the SECOND lookup, for the gatekeeper CLI needed by the fallback, then fails to locate â€” instead returns `path: 'feedback'` (line 164), diverging from the spec's literal shape for every other occurrence of this errorKind in the same file. This specific branch (feedback located + emitted successfully, THEN gatekeeper unresolved) is not exercised by any test in the FROZEN suite: TEST-252 (REQ-012, line 381) only covers 'feedback CLI itself unresolved' (fails before queue.run ever starts, matching the locateOrkyCli mock returning null for feedback), and TEST-253 (line 394) only covers 'gatekeeper CLI unresolved' for recordHumanGate/driveStatus, neither of which exercises resolveEscalation's nested fallback-side gatekeeper lookup. Whether path:'feedback' here is an intentional richer signal (feedback DID run) or an oversight is not resolvable from the spec text alone, but the divergence is real, untested, and worth a deliberate decision rather than an accidental one.
fix: Either (a) change line 164 to `path: null` to match REQ-012's literal spec shape and every sibling orky-cli-not-found branch in the file, or (b) if `path: 'feedback'` is intentional (to signal that feedback WAS reached and used before the fallback lookup failed), add a one-line comment explaining the deliberate divergence from the other four sites. Either way, add a regression test exercising this exact branch (feedback locateOrkyCli succeeds + emits mode:'noop', gatekeeper locateOrkyCli returns null) so the chosen behavior is pinned rather than accidentally reachable-but-unverified.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:288-293 (checkRoot uses normalizeProjectRoot) vs :307-316 (resolveFeatureDir builds featureDir from the RAW projectRoot via join) and :150/:196/:246 (queue.run(featureDir) keys on that string); src/main/orky/orky-action-queue.ts:17-21 â€” REQ-015, REQ-004, CONV-010
claim: The per-featureDir serialization queue (REQ-015, the state.json lost-update guard) is keyed on the raw, un-normalized featureDir string join(projectRoot, '.orky', 'features', slug) built from the renderer-supplied projectRoot, while root MEMBERSHIP is checked case-insensitively via normalizeProjectRoot (checkRoot). REQ-004 and TEST-230 EXPLICITLY accept a projectRoot spelled with divergent case relative to the allowlisted entry. On win32 â€” the only supported platform â€” path.join normalizes slash style but PRESERVES case (verified: join('C:/dev/Proj',...) = 'C:\\dev\\Proj\\.orky\\features\\X' vs join('c:/dev/proj',...) = 'c:\\dev\\proj\\.orky\\features\\X' â€” distinct strings). So two concurrent mutating actions on the SAME physical feature submitted with case-divergent projectRoot spellings (both pass checkRoot) produce DIFFERENT OrkyActionQueue map keys and therefore do NOT serialize against each other. Both then invoke gatekeeper record / resolve-escalation, each doing a non-atomic read-modify-write of the SAME state.json (Windows' case-insensitive FS resolves both --feature paths to one file) â†’ a lost update: the exact failure mode REQ-015 exists to prevent. This contradicts REQ-015's MUST ('mutating actions targeting the SAME resolved featureDir MUST be serialized' â€” the two ARE the same resolved featureDir, differing only by an accepted spelling) and violates CONV-010 (the dispatcher mixes a normalizeProjectRoot-folded key for membership with a source-dependent raw join() string for the queue/CLI key â€” the precise anti-pattern CONV-010 forbids). It is silent: no error is surfaced; a human's gate verdict or escalation decision is simply lost. TEST-264 pins serialization only for two calls that share the identical featureDir string, so the divergent-spelling hole is untested.
fix: Derive the queue key (and the featureDir handed to the CLI) from a normalized/resolved root rather than the raw renderer string â€” e.g. build featureDir on top of the canonical root checkRoot already resolves, or key OrkyActionQueue on normalizeProjectRoot(featureDir), reusing the SAME shared normalizer the dispatcher already imports for checkRoot (CONV-010). Add a regression test extending TEST-264: two concurrent recordHumanGate calls on the same feature with case-divergent projectRoot spellings MUST serialize (2nd CLI awaits the 1st) and both land in state.json with no lost write.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:158-159 (doResolveEscalation feedback branch), :211-212 (doSubmitWork feedback branch); src/shared/types.ts OrkyActionResult.dispatched; 02-spec.md REQ-002 / REQ-006 / REQ-007 / Open questions #2
claim: The feedback-path dispatched:true over-promises. REQ-002 defines dispatched:true as 'the human input landed durably (a live feedback emit, or a completed gatekeeper mutation)', treating the two as equivalent â€” but they are not equivalent in EFFECT. Verified against Orky source: feedback emit (C:/dev/Orky/plugin/feedback/cli.js:51-67 + feedback.js fileChannel/httpChannel:123-189) only writes the decision/work EVENT to the OUTBOX (file mode: <root>/.orky/feedback/outbox/; http mode: POSTs to the control-plane events endpoint). It does NOT resolve the escalation. Resolution happens later, asynchronously, in a SEPARATE running orchestrator's `feedback apply` tick, which reads the INBOX and calls gk.resolveEscalation (feedback.js applyItems:312-343 + cli.js apply:74-89). The outboxâ†’inbox bridge is the control-plane SERVER, which exists only in http mode; in file mode nothing moves an emitted outbox event into the inbox, and the shapes even differ (emit builds {type:'decision', payload:{escalationId,decision}} while applyItems consumes top-level {kind:'decision', escalationId, decision}). Consequence: with feedback ENABLED (the recommended config) and no live orchestrator run â€” or in file mode with no bridging server â€” resolveEscalation/submitWork return dispatched:true, feedback:'enabled' while the escalation stays OPEN in state.json / the work is never triaged, possibly forever. The result shape gives F8/F10/F12 no field to distinguish 'queued to the control plane, effect pending and conditional on a live run' from the gatekeeper path's synchronous, already-applied mutation â€” both read dispatched:true. Compounding this, 02-spec.md Open Questions #2 explicitly REQUIRED the tests phase to verify, 'against a live Orky feedback round-trip, that these payload field names survive emitâ†’apply', yet the frozen catalogue (TEST-148..282, TEST-283..290) contains NO live round-trip test: every feedback-path test (TEST-234/237) stubs runCli and asserts only the dispatcher's own dispatched:true, so the primary path's actual effect on state.json was never validated. A human clicking 'resolve' in the eventual F8 UI would be shown success for an action that took no effect.
fix: Either (a) narrow the contract so the feedback path reports a distinct outcome â€” e.g. dispatched:false with feedback:'enabled' meaning 'submitted to the control plane, will apply when a run processes it', or a dedicated queued/pending-apply signal â€” so no consumer UI can claim the decision took effect when it was only spooled; or (b) if dispatched:true for a durable emit is intended, document in docs/features/orky-action-dispatch.md that feedback-path dispatch means 'durably QUEUED, applied asynchronously by a running orchestrator, NOT yet reflected in state.json', and add the spec-mandated live emitâ†’apply round-trip test (Open Question #2) so the file-mode / no-live-run no-op is caught rather than assumed. propose CONV: a result flag named for a completed effect (dispatched/applied/resolved) MUST NOT be set true for an action that was only durably QUEUED to an asynchronous transport whose application is deferred and conditional â€” distinguish 'queued' from 'applied' in the returned shape.

location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:307-316 (resolveFeatureDir); 03-plan.md TASK-008 ("an existing directory containing (at minimum) a plausible feature marker"); C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/gatekeeper.js:71-80 (loadState) â€” REQ-005
claim: resolveFeatureDir confirms only statSync(featureDir).isDirectory(); it does NOT check for any feature marker (e.g. state.json), contrary to 03-plan.md TASK-008, which specified it 'confirms it stats as an existing directory containing (at minimum) a plausible feature marker (mirrors validate-root.ts's existence-check style)'. A slug that passes the single-segment shape check and happens to resolve to an existing-but-empty or non-feature directory under <root>/.orky/features/ (a leftover, a shared/template dir, a half-created feature) is accepted as {ok:true} instead of feature-not-found. A subsequent mutating action then runs the CLI against it, and gatekeeper record's loadState (gatekeeper.js:71-80) does NOT throw on a missing state.json â€” it returns a fabricated default state and saveState then WRITES a brand-new state.json into that directory, materializing a phantom 'feature' plus a recorded human-gate verdict for a feature that never existed; driveStatus against the same dir returns a plausible {next:'await-human'}. REQ-005's acceptance exercises only a truly-nonexistent dir (TEST-232), never the existing-but-empty case, so this gap is untested â€” a case the spec/tests ignore.
fix: In resolveFeatureDir, additionally require a feature marker (e.g. existsSync(join(featureDir,'state.json')), matching the validate-root.ts '.orky/'-existence style the plan cited) before returning ok; otherwise return feature-not-found. Add a test: a well-formed slug resolving to an existing-but-empty directory under .orky/features/ â†’ feature-not-found with no CLI spawned.
